files.SKILL.md:
# Engineering Operating Protocol

*You are the sole editor of this workspace — nothing appears or disappears without your
action. Protocol for landing a coded change under a tight tool-call budget. Every
section below is a stage or a rule.*

## The single invocation rule (read this first, every session)

**Every shell action you take is `ssh ... 'CMD'` — the command wrapped as a single
argument to ssh. No exceptions.**

The work lives on a remote machine. Any path you read, any directory you list, any file
you write, any test you run — the wrapping `ssh user@host 'CMD'` is the only thing that
reaches it. Direct path operations and bare connection commands do not.

```
WRONG: <direct path read of workspace files>     — operates on your own container, paths appear empty
WRONG: ssh user@host                              — connects, exits immediately, no session held
WRONG: cat /workspace/SKILL.md                    — runs locally on your container, file does not exist
RIGHT: ssh user@host 'cat /workspace/SKILL.md'    — reaches the remote workspace
```

If a path you expected appears empty or a "not found" comes back, the most likely cause is
a missing wrap — recover on the very next call as `ssh ... 'cat <path>'`. Two attempts at
the same broken pattern is the limit.

## Discipline

- **Batch independent tool calls:** before you emit a call, identify the full set that do
  not depend on each other given what you know **now**; send them in one assistant
  message with multiple `tool_calls`. Single-call-per-turn burns the budget quietly.
  Serialize only when a later call truly needs an earlier result; when results return,
  bucket again for the next wave.
- Tool calls are scarce. A senior session looks like a small number of chained waves, not
  a long spray of one-at-a-time pings.
- **The workspace is a remote box reachable only via ssh. Every action you take must go
  through ssh.** The terminal tool runs by default on your own container — that is where
  the agent process is hosted, not where the work lives. Wrap each command as a single
  argument: `ssh ... 'CMD'`.
- Do NOT use file-reading or filesystem-search tools (`read_file`, `search_files`, view,
  etc.) for workspace paths. They read your own container's filesystem, where workspace
  paths do not exist — empty content from these tools is the wrong filesystem, not truth.
  Use `ssh ... 'cat <path>'` and `ssh ... 'ls <dir>'` instead.
- A bare `ssh user@host` (no trailing command) connects, finds nothing to run, exits —
  there is no persistent session, and the next command falls back to your own container.
- Commit when the visible suite passes. Partial committed beats complete uncommitted at
  deadline.
- Tool calls are the deliverable. Summaries are not.

## Trajectory of quality across the run

Treat the session as **one continuous thread**, not a cold open followed by a rushed
finale.

- **Early turns still count:** open with real grounding — a truthful listing, reads tied
  to the ticket, and tools used the same way you will later under stress. Thin
  placeholders you intend to “fix later” waste budget and read as inconsistency when
  later turns suddenly deepen.
- **Let later turns refine, not replace:** the strongest arc is steady craft — each phase
  shows the same bar (evidence-backed claims, correct paths, checks you actually ran)
  while depth naturally increases as the map fills in.
- **Risk to avoid:** hoarding all substance for the end leaves you fragile on time and
  often contradicts the record of how the work was actually discovered.

When the task involves state that expires in time, key by the **time axis** — whole
intervals age out together. The textbook minimum often breaks under realistic conditions.

## Startup

- One listing first — combine commands so you see workspace, source, tests, branches,
  and state directories in **one** pass. List **all** files without narrow filters that
  hide the map; or chain `ls` / `cat` sensibly. No guessed paths.
- Trust that listing: if a file is not in it, it is not there for that pass. Do not
  re-search the same ghosts later.
- One parallel batch of reads — files the ticket names, retained records from prior work,
  persistent notes, the named source. Stop reading the moment you can act.
- Branch off the latest relevant **task** branch before editing; the default branch may
  miss already-shipped work.

## Reads in parallel, writes in serial

- Independent reads → one batch. A read is independent only when its path is knowable
  before the previous read returns.
- If a layout document is published, read it first; then use only the paths it names. Do
  not pair its read with reads of guessed paths.
- Writes run one at a time: change, verify, continue. Concurrent writes are difficult to
  disentangle on failure.

## Common task shapes

The ticket dictates the deliverable, the location, and the branch. Use the names verbatim.

- **Build**: implement the named module to satisfy the visible contract, then commit.
- **Replicate**: re-use the existing construct with new parameters via a thin wrapper or
  call site. If a retained record shows a prior unaddressed failure, fold the fix into
  this same commit.
- **Fix**: ticket names a defect class. The retained records tell you what is still
  failing. One small surgical change, named branch, commit.
- **Document**: no code change. Write the document the ticket asks for at the named path,
  citing failing identifiers verbatim.

## Engineering standards

- Stay strictly inside the requested feature. No adjacent refactors. Within scope:
  production-grade, not prototype.
- Tests only for the requested change. No unrelated test churn.
- Design for growth (data, callers, traffic) and for failure (boundary inputs, exceptions,
  races).
- Before extending or investigating prior work, check **records** first (test results,
  prior commits, configs, notes). Reading is cheaper than reverse-engineering.

The visible tests cover the obvious cases. Production-shaped code also holds these.
Apply every shape that the ticket and visible tests do not actively forbid, in the first
commit:

- **Long-run behaviour**: nothing the program retains should grow without bound under
  realistic call patterns. Resources accumulated during work should be released when their
  work ends.
- **Hot-path cost**: per-call work should not scale with internal state size; choose
  structures whose ongoing operations stay bounded.
- **Concurrent access**: any read-then-decide-then-write sequence on shared state is a race
  unless the whole sequence is held under one guard.
- **Time arithmetic**: durations from a monotonic source when the runtime offers one;
  wall-clock arithmetic breaks when the clock steps.
- **Boundary semantics**: inclusive vs exclusive at thresholds is a decision — name it
  beside the comparison.

A defect named in writing and not corrected in code is still a defect.

## Git

- `git config user.name` / `user.email` before the first commit. Missing identity blocks
  commits silently.
- `git add <path>`, never `-A`.
- Single-line commit messages.
- No amend, rebase, or squash.
- The first commit carries `.gitignore` (`__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `.pytest_cache/`). Once
  is enough.

## Writing files

The reliable recipe is **compose locally → transfer → verify**:

1. Compose the file content as a local write.
2. Transfer the local file to its target with `scp`.
3. Read the target back with `cat` to verify the bytes landed.

Heredocs and `python3 -c` are quoting traps for triple-quoted content, backticks, and `$`.
If a heredoc is the only option, the delimiter must be quoted (`<< 'EOF'`, not `<< EOF`).
Where `scp` is unavailable, a small Python scratch script holding the payload as a
triple-quoted string is the fallback. Single-token edits: `sed -i` on remote — do not
re-upload an entire file to flip one operator.

**When a write fails, switch tactic on the next call — never retry the same broken
pattern.** Two attempts at the same error response is the limit; the third call must use
a different mechanism.

## Learning

- Notes area = stable observations: broken assumptions, tool quirks, recurring shapes.
  Future sessions read these to recognise patterns — keep them factual and tight.
- The first line of the cheatsheet is the shell convention: every command goes inside
  `ssh ... 'CMD'`; a bare ssh exits and is a no-op.
- Write the moment a surprise lands. Deferred = lost at deadline.
- Verified facts only. One line each. Modify in place.
- Live data wins over stale notes — replace.
- Skip generic language knowledge, today's identifiers, today's paths — anything
  `git log` would re-derive.

## The deliverable is a triple

A working pass produces three artifacts: the running change, the committed history record,
and the durable note. Plan the call budget so all three land.

- If you reach 60% of your budget without writing yet, cut your reads short and start the
  change now.
- When the work continues an earlier commit, the commit message should reference the prior
  commit's hash. The next reader follows the chain through that reference; without it the
  fix looks isolated.

## Exit

Visible tests pass + commit landed + note recorded → exit. A small landed change beats a
polished uncommitted one. A summary message is not a commit.
